paper_title: Reward Prediction with Factorized World States

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Agents must infer action outcomes and select actions that maximize a reward signal indicating how close the goal is to being reached. Supervised learning of reward models could introduce biases inherent to training data, limiting generalization to novel goals and environments. In this paper, we investigate whether well-defined world state representations alone can enable accurate reward prediction across domains. To address this, we introduce StateFactory, a factorized representation method that transforms unstructured observations into a hierarchical object-attribute structure using language models. This structured representation allows rewards to be estimated naturally as the semantic similarity between the current state and the goal state under hierarchical constraint. Overall, the compact representation structure induced by StateFactory enables strong reward generalization capabilities. We evaluate on RewardPrediction, a new benchmark dataset spanning five diverse domains and comprising 2,454 unique action-observation trajectories with step-wise ground-truth rewards. Our method shows promising zero-shot results against both VLWM-critic and LLM-as-a-Judge reward models, achieving 60% and 8% lower EPIC distance, respectively. Furthermore, this superior reward quality successfully translates into improved agent planning performance, yielding success rate gains of +21.64% on AlfWorld and +12.40% on ScienceWorld over reactive system-1 policies and enhancing system-2 agent planning. Project Page: https://statefactory.github.io

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: We are interested in building agents with planning capabilities that generalize across different goals and environments in zero-shot. Many real-world scenarios do not allow agents to perform massive trial-and-error to obtain explicit reward signals, making model-free approaches, e.g., reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) [Guo et al., 2025a] not applicable. This limitation necessitates world models [Ha and Schmidhuber, 2018, Richens et al., 2025] that predict the consequences of actions as future world states to guide policy learning [Hafner et al., 2023] or plan search [LeCun, 2022, Hansen et al., 2023]. To leverage the state predictions of world models, agents need to evaluate to what extent predicted state aligns with the goal, typically via scalar reward, and then maximize it [M. Moerland et al., 2023]. Our work focuses on accurately predicting such rewards with effective generalization. One direct approach is to train the models [Chen et al., 2025] or reward prediction heads on top of world models [Hafner et al., 2023] using task-specific supervision. However, explicit supervision may introduce biases and overfitting [Di Langosco et al., 2022, Gao et al., 2023, Casper et al., 2023, Sharma et al., 2023], limiting generalization to the novel environments. promising alternative is to estimate task progress by measuring the semantic distance between the current state and the desired goal state [Liu et al., 2022, Schaul et al., 2015], effectively avoiding explicit reward modeling and enabling zero-shot planning [Ma et al., 2023a, Wang et al., 2023a]. There are two bottlenecks in using world-state distances as reward signals. First, constructing an abstract state representation space [M. Moerland et al., 2023] whose geometry accurately reflects task progress [Trott et al., 2019, Wu et al., 2018] is nontrivial. Prior successes leveraged representations from visual foundation models, such as DINO-WM [Zhou et al., 2024], JEPA-WMs [Terver et al., 2025], and RoboCLIP [Sontakke et al., 2023]. Yet, how to extend from low-level continuous planning to highlevel language-based agent planning [Wang et al., 2024a, Luketina et al., 2019, Cao et al., 2024], especially for procedural tasks [Chen et al., 2025] that require stronger semantic and temporal abstraction [Sutton, 1998], remains an open challenge. Second, for agents that operate in text space, rigorous evaluation of reward quality (especially in terms of finegrained, step-wise proximity to the goal state) is difficult due to the lack of suitable benchmarks. Existing datasets mainly focus on sparse, outcome-oriented rewards, which can make it difficult to systematically assess quality of rewards and how they guide the planning process. We address both challenges by introducing new state representation method and dedicated benchmark for rigorous reward evaluation. Specifically, we propose StateFactory, semantic factorization framework that factorizes Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx world states into hierarchical objectattribute structures. Within this framework, unstructured observations are decomposed into atomic semantic factors using large language models. The reward signals are then naturally estimated by measuring semantic similarity between the current and goal states through the hierarchical routing. This process effectively distills the unstructured observations into compact abstractions, thereby improving the zero-shot reward generalization. To enable fine-grained evaluation of reward prediction, we introduce RewardPrediction, benchmark spanning five interactive environments: AlfWorld [Shridhar et al., 2020] for robotics planning, ScienceWorld [Wang et al., 2022] for scientific reasoning, TextWorld [Côté et al., 2018] for text-based games, WebShop [Yao et al., 2022a] for website navigation, and BlocksWorld [Valmeekam et al., 2023a] In total, there are 2,454 unique for classical planning. trajectories, each with step-wise actionobservation pairs and scalar rewards, allowing any predicted reward to be evaluated via the EPIC distance [Gleave et al., 2020] to the ground-truth rewards. Overall, the representation structure in StateFactory demonstrates strong reward generalization capabilities. While supervised reward models exhibit 138% increase in reward prediction error in novel domains on RewardPrediction, StateFactory achieves superior zero-shot performance, reducing EPIC distance by 60% and 8% compared to VLWM-critic and LLM-as-a-Judge, respectively. These improvements in reward quality turn directly into enhanced agent planning performance, yielding success rate gains of +21.64% on AlfWorld and +12.40% on ScienceWorld over reactive system-1 policies, while consistently strengthening system-2 agent planning. The main contributions of this work are summarized: We introduce RewardPrediction benchmark, covering 2,454 unique trajectories with step-wise ground truth reward across five text-based environments. We propose StateFactory, new representation method to transform the flat text descriptions to the structured hierarchies for the generalizable reward prediction. We demonstrate that StateFactory representation is able to enhance agent planning performance when combined with world model and action proposal.

Figure 1 provides an overview of the benchmark task, and Figure 2 presents representative examples of different domains. 2.1 Formulation Since we are interested in building objective-driven goalreaching agents, we consider Goal-Augmented Markov Decision Process (GA-MDP) [Liu et al., 2022] with Partial Observation [Kaelbling et al., 1998] constraints: ℳ = 𝒮 , 𝒜, 𝒪, 𝒯 , ℛ, 𝒢 , 𝜙, Ω. Here 𝒮, 𝒜, and 𝒪 respectively denote state, action, and the observation space. 𝒯 is the underlying transition function of the environment. The function Ω : 𝒮 𝒪 maps underlying states into agent observations. Each observation 𝑜𝑡 usually partially contains the information of the ground truth states 𝑠𝑡. ℛ denotes reward function and 𝒢 is the goal spacewe consider language goal in this work. Given textual goal description 𝑔 𝒢 and an agent that reaches 𝑠𝑡, the corresponding reward ℛ(𝑠𝑡 , 𝑔) should measure how close is the agent from achieving the goal. With the mapping 𝜙 : 𝒢 𝒮 that interprets the goals and translates them into state space, it should have ℛ(𝑠𝑡 , 𝑔) = 1 if the goal is reached, i.e., when 𝑠𝑡 𝜙(𝑔) 𝜖 with small 𝜖 [Andrychowicz et al., 2017]. To allow representing the richer task progress information, we adopt the distance-to-goal reward [Trott et al., 2019, Liu et al., 2022]: ℛ(𝑠𝑡 , 𝑔) = 1 𝑠𝑡 𝜙(𝑔). (1) Prediction Task. At each time 𝑡, an agent 𝜋 perceives an observation 𝑜𝑡, takes an action 𝑎𝑡, and obtains the reward 𝑟𝑡. We can collect offline trajectories of agent interacting with environments to achieve goals, each expressed as Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx Figure 2. RewardPrediction Benchmark Overview. Representative trajectories across five diverse domains. Each column displays task instance with actions, observations, and the corresponding ground-truth task progress score (𝑅 [0, 1]) at key time steps. 𝑡=0 𝑔, [𝑜𝑡 , 𝑎𝑡 , 𝑟𝑡]𝑛 . Based on collected goals and trajectories, we define the RewardPrediction task  (Fig. 1)  , which requires reward predictor 𝑓pred to estimate ground truth rewards based on the goal to achieve, past observations, and action history: 𝑓pred(𝑔, 𝑜[0:𝑡], 𝑎[0:𝑡]) ˆ𝑟𝑡 . (2) Iterating over every step, we obtain sequence of predicted rewards [ˆ𝑟𝑡]𝑛 𝑡=0. Prediction of each step could be independent (stateless), but alternatively, 𝑓pred is also allowed to keep and access intermediate results from previous steps in the trajectory (stateful), e.g., allowing them to maintain memory of belief states for more efficient prediction. Evaluation Metric. We adopt the Equivalent PolicyInvariant Comparison (EPIC) distance [Gleave et al., 2020, Frick et al., 2024] between ground truth rewards and predicted rewards as the metric. EPIC distance is an approach to quantify the difference between reward functions, providing policy-invariant measure that preserves the fine-grained magnitude information essential for robust planning. Drawing on Rocamonde et al. [2023], which simplifies EPIC to closed-form function of Pearson correlation for state-only rewards, we formulate the metric as: 𝐷EPIC = (cid:113) 1 2 1 𝜌( ˆ𝑅, 𝑅), (3) where 𝜌 represents Pearson correlation, the predicted rewards and 𝑅 = [𝑟𝑡]𝑛 ˆ𝑅 = [ˆ𝑟𝑡]𝑛 𝑡=0 are 𝑡=0 are the ground truths. We report average EPIC distance across all trajectories as the final performance. 2.2 Implementation To ensure robustness across diverse physical and semantic landscapes, we construct this benchmark by collecting task-balanced interaction trajectories directly from suite of five real text-based environments. To mitigate heuristic reward hacking, we employ paired data construction strategy. Specifically, each instance consists of positivenegative pair tailored to the same task. The positive anchor is derived from an expert trajectory with deterministic progress gradients[Yang et al., 2024] (𝑟𝑡 = 𝑡/𝑇) [Ziakas and Russo, 2025]. Crucially, we augment these sequences with random interaction steps at the boundaries, inherit their native environment rewards, to decouple trajectory length from reward signals. Complementing this, the negative baseline is generated via random policy to represent failure trajectories (𝑟𝑡 = 0). This structural coupling forces the model to explicitly distinguish meaningful semantic progress from stochastic noise within the same task context. The final evaluation set comprises 2,454 trajectories, as illustrated in Figure 2. For the supervised training stage, we scale up data generation to produce approximately 350,000 trajectories. AlfWorld [Shridhar et al., 2020]. benchmark for embodied household instruction following requiring agents to map high-level goals (e.g., put hot mug in cabinet) to sequences of executable step-level actions (e.g., take mug, heat mug, and put mug in cabinet). Our dataset covers 3 Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx BlocksWorld [Valmeekam et al., 2023a]. classical planning benchmark for evaluating multi-step spatial reasoning and stack manipulation under strict physical constraints. Tasks involve reconfiguring block arrangements to match target state (e.g., stack block on top of block B) using atomic actions like pick up, unstack, and put down. We subsampled official instances and utilized PDDL-totext translator to ground symbolic states (e.g., on(a,b)) into dense natural language descriptions. This process bridges the gap between symbolic logic and linguistic reasoning, constructing 500 trajectories that challenge the agents ability to track long-horizon dependencies. 3 Reward Prediction Methods In this section, we explore different approaches to solving the RewardPrediction benchmark. We introduce two families of methods: representation-free methods and representation-based methods, as illustrated in Fig. 3. The key distinction lies in whether the reward predictor constructs explicit world state representations (belief states) and explicit goal interpretations (approximating 𝜙). Despite architectural differences, methods can also be categorized by whether they leverage the RewardPrediction training set, resulting in two regimes: supervised predictors and zero-shot predictors. 3.1 Representation-free Methods 3.1.1 Finetuned Language Models Inspired by reward models used in Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) [Ouyang et al., 2022] and the self-supervised critic used in the Vision Language World Model (VLWM) [Chen et al., 2025], we train language models with linear head as 𝑓pred to predict the reward. Specifically, we generate paired samples from the training set of RewardPrediction, where the positive sample has reward that is higher than the negative sample. We then train the language model with contrastive ranking loss [Chen et al., 2025] with reward centering term [Naik et al., 2024]. The reward model here is fully stateless. Given input goal description and (partial) trajectory at each time step, it predicts the reward independently. 3.1.2 LLM-as-a-Judge Prompting This approach leverages prompted LLM-as-a-Judge [Zheng et al., 2023] as generative reward predictor [Guo et al., 2025b, Gunjal et al., 2025, Whitehouse et al., 2025]. We provide goal and trajectory to an LLM, with additional guidance describing the reward prediction task, to instruct it to respond with scaler reward on scale of 0 to 1. See Appendix Table 8 for the full prompt. In addition to this simple stateless baseline, the flexibility of prompting allow us to implement stateful variant: the LLM is instructed Figure 3. Two paradigms of reward prediction. Unlike representationfree methods that regress rewards directly from raw inputs (top), representation-based frameworks derive progress signals by measuring the alignment between factorized state representations 𝑠𝑡 and goal interpretations 𝑔𝑡 (bottom). all six standard interaction types, including Pick & Place, Examine, Cool, Heat, Clean, and Movable, comprising total of 672 expert trajectories. ScienceWorld [Wang et al., 2022]. complex environment simulating elementary science experiments across topics like thermodynamics and life sciences. It requires agents to perform multi-step causal reasoning, such as determine the melting point of lead via tool-use actions like move lead to stove, turn on stove, and focus on thermometer. We curated 382 trajectories from the test. TextWorld [Côté et al., 2018]. generative framework for procedural text-adventure games requiring multi-step reasoning. We focused on the quests involving complex object manipulation and the lock-and-key puzzles (e.g., retrieve the shoe from the safe and place it on the table). These tasks require the sequence-dependent actions such as take keycard, unlock safe with the keycard, and put shoe on the table. We utilized the native engine to procedurally generate 300 trajectories focusing on these state-change puzzles. WebShop [Yao et al., 2022a]. scalable simulation of e-commerce website navigation requiring agents to find products matching specific attributes (e.g., looking for blue running shoe size 9). Actions simulate real browser interactions, including keyword searches and selecting options (e.g., search[blue shoe], click[size-9], click[buy]). Unlike other domains, we leveraged human demonstrations to capture realistic navigation nuances. From pool of successful human trajectories, we subsampled highquality instances to serve as positive anchors, forming total of 600 trajectories. 4 Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx Figure 4. StateFactory Framework. State extraction and goal interpretation are coupled recurrent processes (left). Our approach factorizes states into explicit objects and attributes (right), deriving dense rewards from semantic similarity between ˆ𝑠𝑡 and ˆ𝑔𝑡 . to construct and manage belief state over timea summary as memory that tracks the world state. 3.2 Representation-based Method on the table). 3.2.2 Goal Interpretation (StateFactory) StateFactory predicts task progress by decomposing the GA-MDP ℳ into three integrated layers: world state transition, state extraction (ˆ𝑠𝑡), and goal interpretation ( ˆ𝑔𝑡). As illustrated in Fig. 4, raw observations 𝑜𝑡 = Ω(𝑠𝑡) are distilled into structured object-attribute states ˆ𝑠𝑡, while the textual goal 𝑔 𝒢 is iteratively grounded into dynamic goal state ˆ𝑔𝑡. The reward signal is derived via semantic alignment: ˆ𝑟𝑡 = sim( ˆ𝑔𝑡 , ˆ𝑠𝑡). This factorization ensures zero-shot rewards remain grounded in physical transitions while staying responsive to specific goal description. 3.2.1 State Extraction Effective goal interpretation must bridge abstract instructions with evolving physical states. Traditional methods typically fix the goal representation at initialization [Mahmoudieh et al., 2022, Xie et al., 2023], which often creates an illusion of progress as they fail to adapt to environmental changes during task execution [Xue et al., 2025]. In contrast to static methods, StateFactory treats goal interpretation as an iterative, state-aware process. As illustrated in the blue pathway of Figure 4, we employ function 𝑓goal to dynamically update the goal state ˆ𝑔𝑡 based on the goal description 𝑔 𝒢 and the current context: ˆ𝑔𝑡 = 𝑓goal(𝑔, ˆ𝑔𝑡1, ˆ𝑠𝑡 , 𝑜𝑡 , 𝑎𝑡1). StateFactory treats state extraction as recurrent, goal-conditioned update process. sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: In this section, we evaluate StateFactory across diverse planning domains to address the following research questions: 1) whether our zero-shot approach provides more accurate reward signals than baselines; 2) whether it generalizes better to new domains compared to supervised models; 3) how structural granularity contributes to the performance improvement; 4) how robust the framework is to different design choices (e.g., embeddings, reasoning capacity); and 5) whether these signals can effectively guide online planning to improve success rates. 4.1 Baselines and Setup We standardize experiments on RewardPrediction using gpt-oss-20b for state extraction and all-MiniLM-L6-v2 for alignment. We compare StateFactory against three (1) naive Monotonic distinct categories of baselines: Baseline; (2) supervised approaches, including VLWMcritic [Chen et al., 2025] and other reward models explicitly trained on RewardPrediction; and (3) LLM-as-a-Judge. Full implementation details are provided in Appx. C. 4.2 Main results on RewardPrediction We evaluate reward prediction accuracy using the EPIC distance (𝐷EPIC), where lower values indicate better alignment with ground-truth progress. Table 1 presents comprehensive comparison of StateFactory against both supervised and representation-free baselines on the RewardPrediction benchmark dataset. Supervised Reward Models. Table 1 reveals the severe generalization bottleneck of supervised approaches. While these models achieve exceptional precision within their training domains (e.g., 0.212 in AlfWorld), they exhibit an average error increase of 138% when transferred to unseen tasks. This confirms that supervised critics tend to overfit domain-specific surface patterns rather than learn generalized notion of progress, rendering them less effective for zero-shot generalization. Representation-free Methods. Training-free baselines avoid the overfitting trap but face unique challenges. Inspection of reasoning traces reveals that LLMs implicitly maintain belief state and extract goals at each step, which improves reward prediction. However, constrained by the probabilistic nature of generation, these implicit reward signals remain discrete and volatile. While they perform slightly better in structured domains like TextWorld (where tasks resemble checklist) and BlocksWorld (suitable for reverse engineering), this implicit tracking breaks down in open-ended environments. Representation-based Method. StateFactory addresses the dual challenges of reward generalization and signal continuity. By explicitly decomposing the state space into structured object-attribute representations, our method transforms reward estimation from discrete generation task into continuous semantic distance calculation. The fine-grained hierarchical factorization structure not only effectively mitigates noise in flat text observations, but also enables StateFactory to capture subtle progresses that implicit methods could miss, thus yielding robust estimator 6 Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx Figure 5. StateFactory ablation experiments. We report the EPIC distance (𝐷EPIC) across settings (a-d) and Triplet-based Accuracy for embedding models (e). For metrics, lower 𝐷EPIC and higher Accuracy indicate better performance. Specifically, Triplet-based Accuracy reflects the models capability to enforce smaller distances for positive pairs compared to negative ones. Red hatched bars highlight the best performing configurations. Conversely, blue hatched bars indicate the worst performing ones. that generalizes across different domains without the need of any parameter update (training). As can be observed, StateFactory achieves an average 𝐷EPIC of 0.297, not only superior to the best representation-free baseline (0.322) but also surpassing single-domain supervised models in generalization. Remarkably, this zero-shot performance approaches the fully supervised upper bound trained on the entire dataset (by combining data across all domains). 4.3 Ablation Studies of StateFactory Importance of Abstraction. Transitioning from unstructured observations to extracted textual states yields significant distance reduction (0.57 0.43) in Figure 5(a). This confirms that raw observations contain excessive distractors, whereas state extraction isolates task-relevant information. However, unstructured text remains susceptible to score dilution, where critical state changes are often overshadowed by the total sentence length. Factorization Granularity. As shown in Figure 5(a), while object-centric states mitigate dilution by isolating objects (0.35), they hit precision ceiling due to attribute entanglement. Merging distinct properties into single embedding (e.g., blue mug on the table) creates interference, since the model cannot track the location change clearly because the color information acts as noise. StateFactory overcomes this by refining the granularity to explicit object-attribute states. By decomposing observations into minimal semantic units, our method eliminates semantic interference and achieves the best performance (0.30). Goal Interpretation. To evaluate the fidelity of our dynamic goal interpretation, we compare it against an offline (oracle) baseline. We define an offline setting where final state of the expert trajectory serves as the ground truth goal state and compare it with our online setting where goals are interpreted dynamically from interaction history. As shown in Figure 5(b), the distance between real-time inference and the offline baseline is minimal. The average 𝐷𝐸𝑃𝐼𝐶 increases by only 0.02 when transitioning from the offline (0.28) to the online setting (0.30). This stability suggests that our goal interpretation effectively recovers target requirements. LLM Backbone. We investigate the impact of LLM backbone capabilities on the state factorization, distinguishing between the inference ability (reasoning-enhanced modes) and the parametric scale. As shown in Figure 5(c), both dimensions closely correlate with alignment precision. First, enabling thinking modes yields significant gains: gpt-oss-20b thinking medium improves from 0.36 to 0.30, and qwen3-30B-thinking (0.41) substantially outperforms its standard counterpart (0.55), confirming that the extended inference capability aids in decomposing complex states. 7 Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx Second, we observe that performance generally improves with parameter count within the same model family, showing that reasoning ability is effectively grounded in model size. Consequently, StateFactory is positioned to scale continuously with future advancements in both model size and reasoning strategies. Semantic Embeddings. One advantage of StateFactory is that any text-embedding model can be applied on top of the hierarchical object-attribute structure to compute final reward scores. Here, we investigate the correlation between the discriminative power of the embedding and the performance of StateFactory. This discriminative power is measured by the triplet-based accuracy [Dumpala et al., 2024], derived from triplets (details in Appx. C.5.3) comprising state description (anchor), semantic paraphrase (positive), and contradiction (negative). The metric is defined as the success rate where the embedding correctly assigns higher similarity to the positive pair than to the negative one. As shown in Fig. 5(d) and (e), high triplet accuracy strictly correlates with performance: discriminative models (e.g., all-MiniLM) yield superior alignment (𝐷EPIC 0.30), whereas weaker embedding like gemma-300m (acc= 0.67) fail (𝐷EPIC = 0.45). This finding validates that the effectiveness of StateFactory is driven by the discriminative quality of the embedding space. 4.4 Utility for Agent Planning Quantitative Analysis. We posit that the superior reward prediction capabilities of StateFactory translate directly into enhanced agent planning performance. To verify this, we evaluate the ReAct + StateFactory agent, which integrates our reward signals into the standard ReAct baseline. Unlike the vanilla ReAct agent that relies solely on internal reasoning, our method augments decisionmaking by explicitly ranking top-𝐾 action candidates. As summarized in Table 2, this approach yields robust and consistent gains across all distinct tested domains. Notably, the success rate in AlfWorld improves by 20+ percentage points (34.33% 55.97%), with similar steady climbs observed in BlocksWorld and ScienceWorld. Crucially, we find that explicit reward signals empower the agent to break out of reasoning deadlocks, thereby successfully overcoming the ambiguity of long-horizon planning where pure logic alone typically fails. Qualitative Analysis. As exemplified by the preliminary case study in Table 17, we further investigate whether accurate reward prediction facilitates complex planning within system-2 framework. We implement this by integrating LLM-based action proposals, learned world model [Li et al., 2025], and StateFactory into Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS). Crucially, this reward signal is underpinned by our dynamic goal interpretation and state extraction, which refine abstract intents into granular requirements and trigger instantaneous reward spikes upon object discovery, ensuring simulated trajectories remain grounded in physical evidence. 5 Related Works Goal Conditioned Reinforcement Learning. Accurately measuring task advancement is essential for policy optimization [Yu et al., 2025]. Early approaches relying on manual engineering [Lillicrap et al., 2015, Singh et al., 2019, Andrychowicz et al., 2020] are labor-intensive and notoriously susceptible to the reward hacking [Amodei et al., 2016, Kaufmann et al., 2024]. To alleviate these issues, recent methods leverage human feedback, including the demonstrations and preferences [Christiano et al., 2017, Ouyang et al., 2022, Hong et al., 2024, Xiao et al., 2025], or LLM-synthesized rewards [Ma et al., 2023b, Xie et al., 2023, Wang et al., 2025, Xue et al., 2025]. However, these methods struggle to generalize to novel domains due to their dependence on task-specific supervision [Wolf et al., 2025]. Consequently, the field has shifted towards unsupervised paradigms that extract dense signals directly from pre-trained representations. These approaches can be broadly categorized into two streams based on how they utilize the feature space. The first stream focuses on explicit reward modeling, where scalar signals are derived by quantifying task progress or goal probability. This includes methods such as vision-language alignment [Rocamonde et al., 2023, Sontakke et al., 2023, Alakuĳala et al., 2024], discriminative classification likelihood [Sermanet et al., 2016, Warde-Farley et al., 2018] or temporal distances in passive videos [Sermanet et al., 2018, Liu et al., 2025]. The second stream employs latent world models for implicit reward prediction [Zhou et al., 2024, Terver et al., 2025, Sobal et al., 2025]. Instead of relying on explicit scorers, these methods derive signals by formulating the latent distance to the goal as an intrinsic cost, effectively Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx transforming predictive dynamics into dense progress indicator for planning. State Representation in World Models. The performance of language-based agent learning heavily relies on the fidelity of world representation [Valmeekam et al., 2023b, Wang et al., 2023b]. Existing approaches address this challenge with varying degrees of structural assumpInitial works employ unstructured representations, tion. typically maintaining the state as raw observations [Yao et al., 2022b, Hao et al., 2023]. However, this lack of filtering retains excessive task-irrelevant noise. This information overload obscures critical state changes, making it difficult to track the status of objects consistently. [Valmeekam et al., 2023a]. To better organize these observations, recent objectcentric frameworks decompose environments into discrete entities [Feng et al., 2025, Yoneda et al., 2024]. Yet, these approaches typically treat objects as simple units, failing to separate the objects identity from its changing attributes. Consequently, it becomes difficult to track specific changes (e.g., the rising temperature of an ingredient). This lack of detail hinders the measurement of precise progress required for effective learning [Wang et al., 2024b]. To capture these fine-grained dynamics, recent studies have explored object-attribute representations, which factorize entities into structured attribute-value pairs to support more accurate state tracking [Zhu et al., 2023, Rozanov and Rei, 2025]. Language-based Agent Learning. Similar to physical control, the scope of sequential decision-making extends to digital environments. Here, interactions such as web navigation [Zhou et al., 2023, Deng et al., 2023], operating system control [Zhang et al., 2025a, Xie et al., 2024] and text-based games [Côté et al., 2018, Hausknecht et al., 2020] are formulated as control problems governed by comparable dynamics. Traditionally, agents operate as model-free policies, mapping observations to actions via in-context reasoning [Wei et al., 2022, Yao et al., 2022b, Shinn et al., 2023, Park et al., 2023], yet often struggle with long-horizon consistency and generalization [Wang et al., 2024a, Madaan et al., 2023, Wang and Li, 2023]. To address this, recent work adopts the Model-based RL framework, validating LLMs as implicit world models that simulate text or code transitions [Zhuge et al., 2023, Li et al., 2025]. This predictive capability enables search-based planning (e.g., MCTS) in web agents, outperforming reactive prompting by simulating future states before execution [Gu et al., 2024, Hao et al., 2023, Yao et al., 2023]. Furthermore, CoSPlan [Grover et al., 2025] demonstrates that incorporating incremental scene graph updates allows agents to explicitly track state transitions, enabling them to detect and rectify errors in complex visual sequences. Concurrently, to mitigate exploration bottlenecks, the paradigm is shifting to offline reinforcement learning [Kumar et al., 2020, Levine et al., 2020]. Recent Approaches [Zhang et al., 2025b, Nakano et al., 2021] refine policies directly from sub-optimal offline data without extensive online interaction. This leverages implicit value frameworks [Snell et al., 2022] to distill optimal behaviors from mediocre traces, principle now extended to self-generated agent experiences [Chebotar et al., 2023]. 6 Conclusion In this work, we demonstrate that structured text-based world state representations alone can enable accurate and generalizable reward prediction. We introduce the RewardPrediction benchmark and propose StateFactory, zero-shot framework that achieves superior prediction accuracy by factorizing observations into hierarchical objectattribute structures. Our results show that StateFactory exhibits clear advantages over supervised models and LLM-as-a-Judge baselines across diverse domains. Furthermore, our state-driven signals yield success rate improvements of up to 21.64% for system-1 policies , while qualitative analysis validates the utility of StateFactory for system-2 agent planning by effectively guiding structured search grounded in physical evidence. sections.title: A Benchmark Details and Data Construction
sections.content: A.1 Four-Stage Data Generation Pipeline To ensure high-quality supervision for reward modeling and to mitigate the sparsity of environmental feedback, we constructed the RewardPrediction benchmark using rigorous four-stage data generation pipeline derived strictly from native environment feedback. Positive Trajectory Synthesis with Random Augmentation. Positive trajectories are primarily grounded in absolute expert demonstrations provided by native environments or human experts. To enhance the diversity of the dataset and introduce temporal robustness, we implement Random Padding mechanism: expert sequences are padded with 𝑘 [0, 3] random interaction steps, which are stochastically distributed at both the initiation and termination of the trajectory. This augmentation simulates realistic scenarios where an agent might perform sub-optimal exploration before or after achieving the core task. Ground-Truth Reward Derivation via Hybrid Labeling. (i) Expert Segment Densification: The core expert segment is assigned dense rewards via linear interpolation, formulated as 𝑟𝑡 = 𝑡/𝑇expert. This captures the monotonic progress and the increasing value of states as they approach the goal. (ii) Random Step Grounding: Conversely, the appended random steps strictly inherit their original environmental reward values (typically zero). This ensures the model learns sharp contrast between the dense progress of expert actions and the random exploration. Negative Sample Generation & Strict Filtering Protocol. Negative trajectories are generated via stochastic random policy to represent non-attainment of goals. While random exploration predominantly yields no progress, we enforce Strict Filtering Protocol to eliminate label noise: any random trajectory that inadvertently overlaps with, or matches any sub-segment of, an expert demonstration is immediately discarded. By mitigating the risk of "accidental successes" being labeled as failures, we justify strict ground truth of 𝑟𝑡 1 for all steps in the negative set, establishing robust, flat non-successful baseline. Native Observation, Action Space and Reward. To ensure the maximal fidelity and empirical rigor of the RewardPrediction benchmark, we strictly adhere to principle of minimal intervention across all data modalities. All observations are extracted directly from the native environment without any form of manual modification. Similarly, we maintain the absolute completeness of the action space by requiring the model to operate within the full, unaltered set of admissible candidate actions provided by the environment at each discrete time step, rather than relying on simplified or heuristic-based action subsets. Furthermore, all foundational reward signals utilized in our labeling pipeline are sourced entirely from the environments intrinsic feedback logic. This preservation of unedited signals ensures that our benchmark reflects the authentic complexity of the original task domain. A.2 Evaluation Task Environments To evaluate our method across diverse set of challenges, ranging from household navigation to scientific reasoning, we incorporated five distinct interactive environments: AlfWorld: AlfWorld is an embodied text-based environment covering tasks like cleaning, heating, and cooling objects, challenging agents to ground high-level language instructions into sequential navigation and manipulation actions within simulated domestic scenes. ScienceWorld: ScienceWorld simulates scientific experiments across interconnected rooms, demanding deep understanding of physics, scientific causality, and multi-step logical reasoning to interact with diverse objects. WebShop: WebShop is massive simulated e-commerce environment built on real-world Amazon data, testing the agents ability to process noisy web text, match complex multi-attribute user intents, and perform long-horizon navigation to successfully complete purchases. TextWorld: TextWorld is procedural text-adventure framework with partially observable environments, requiring agents to utilize memory and map abstract textual clues to sequential puzzle-solving strategies. BlocksWorld: BlocksWorld is fundamental planning environment where the agent must logically unstack and restack colored blocks on table to reach specific target configuration, testing basic spatial and sequential reasoning under strict physical constraints. 16 Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx A.3 Domain-Specific Sourcing and Preprocessing We tailored the sampling and filtering strategy for each domain to ensure dataset quality and balance: AlfWorld (Uniform Sampling): We randomly sampled 336 tasks from the training set, representing approximately 10% of the total data. To prevent task bias, we ensured uniform distribution across the six distinct task types, resulting in exactly 56 positive-negative trajectory pairs per task type. ScienceWorld (Validity Filtering): We selected 191 trajectories from the test set. During selection, we explicitly filtered out specific tasks (e.g., task-4-grow-fruit and task-4-grow-plant) where the native oracle could not reliably reach task progress of 1.0 due to environmental limitations. Additionally, we clipped terminal failure scores (e.g., -100) to 0 to maintain reward boundedness. WebShop (Human Demonstrations): We leveraged human demonstrations as the expert policy. From total pool of 711 successful human trajectories, we randomly subsampled 300 high-quality instances to serve as positive samples, ensuring diverse coverage of e-commerce navigation behaviors. TextWorld (Procedural Generation): We utilized the native engine to procedurally generate 150 unique textadventure instances, focusing on cooking and treasure-hunting quests that require multi-step reasoning. BlocksWorld (Subsampling & PDDL): Sourced from the BlocksWorld suite, we randomly subsampled 250 instances from the official 500-instance dataset. We utilized the authorized PDDL-to-text translator to convert symbolic states (e.g., on(a, b)) into natural language descriptions, ensuring consistency with other environments. Table 4 and Table 5 visualize representative positivenegative trajectory pair from the AlfWorld domain, illustrating how our pipeline converts sparse environmental feedback into dense, semantically-rich supervision signal. This sample exemplifies our hybrid supervision approach: Expert Segments (𝑡 = 0 to 5): The core sequence follows an optimal expert policy. As per our methodology, these steps are assigned monotonically increasing dense rewards (from 0.17 to 1.00) via linear interpolation, providing granular gradient that captures task progress. Random Augmentation (𝑡 = 6): The final step is an appended random action (random_post). Crucially, this step strictly inherits the native environment success signal (𝑅 = 1.0), ensuring the model learns to maintain the success state even amidst non-expert exploration. Despite the agents active interaction with the environment (e.g., opening drawers), the actions fail to advance the task state toward the goal. Experimental Implementation Details C.1 Monotonic Baseline (Rule-based Heuristic) The Monotonic Baseline serves as non-parametric, rule-based reference to evaluate the necessity of semantic reward modeling. In this configuration, we bypass environmental feedback and instead apply naive temporal heuristic to both positive and negative trajectories. Specifically, for any trajectory of length 𝑇, the reward at each time step 𝑡 is assigned via linear interpolation: 𝑟𝑡 = 𝑡/𝑇. By enforcing this strictly increasing progress signal regardless of the actual state-action semantics, this baseline tests whether simple "time-equals-progress" assumption can suffice for the task. It provides lower bound for our evaluation, highlighting the limitations of ignoring the distinction between expert goal-oriented actions and aimless exploration. C.2 Supervised Reward Model In this section, we detail the dataset curation, model architecture, and training protocols employed for the reward model (RM) baselines. C.2.1 Dataset Construction and Input Format We curated preference datasets across five distinct interactive environments: AlfWorld, TextWorld, ScienceWorld, Blocks World, and WebShop. We collected 35,000 preference pairs for each environment, yielding cumulative dataset of 175,000 pairs. Each sample comprises raw text trajectory containing high-level goal, sequence of actions, and corresponding state observations. Deviating from standard instruction-tuning formats, we eschew separate system prompts; instead, the input is fed as flattened interaction history. The input format is structured as follows: <GOAL> [Task Description] </GOAL> <ACTION> [Action1] </ACTION> <STATE> [Observation1] </STATE> ... We trained six distinct reward models: five task-specific models (35k pairs each) and one comprehensive model (All-Combined) trained on the union of all datasets (175k pairs). C.2.2 Model Architecture and LoRA Configuration We employ Qwen2.5-1.5B [Hui et al., 2024] as the backbone architecture. To manage computational costs efficiently and mitigate potential catastrophic forgetting, we utilize Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) [Hu et al., 2022] in lieu of full-parameter fine-tuning. The reward model is implemented by appending scalar classification head to the base model. LoRA adapters are applied to all linear layers with rank of 𝑟 = 16 and an alpha scaling factor of 𝛼 = 32. The training objective minimizes the negative log-likelihood of the preferred trajectory rankings: ℒ(𝜃) = E(𝑦𝑤 ,𝑦𝑙 )𝒟 (cid:2)log 𝜎 (cid:0)𝑟𝜃(𝑦𝑤) 𝑟𝜃(𝑦𝑙)(cid:1) (cid:3) (13) where 𝑦𝑤 and 𝑦𝑙 denote the chosen and rejected trajectories, respectively. C.2.3 Training Setup and Hyperparameters All models were implemented using the Hugging Face trl library. To accommodate the extensive context requirements of agent interaction histories, we set the maximum sequence length to 8192 tokens and utilized mixed-precision training (BF16) with gradient checkpointing to optimize memory efficiency. As detailed in Table 7, the hardware infrastructure varied due to resource availability, ranging from dual NVIDIA RTX A6000 configurations to clusters of NVIDIA A100 GPUs. Consequently, while the effective batch size and training duration varied by task, the core optimization hyperparameters  (Table 6)  were kept constant to ensure fair comparison. Models were generally trained for 3 epochs, with the exception of WebShop and the Combined model, which were trained for 2 epochs. All evaluating models are configured with context window of 8,192 tokens to accommodate long-horizon trajectories without information loss. Furthermore, we set the sampling temperature to 0.01 across all variants to enforce near-deterministic outputs, thereby ensuring the high stability and reproducibility of our reward signals. To systematically investigate the impact of reasoning on reward judgment, we employ two distinct control mechanisms: 1. Prompt-Level Reasoning Ablation (e.g., Qwen-2.5-14B). For general-purpose LLMs, we modulate reasoning capabilities through explicit system-level instructions, allowing us to isolate the effects of internal Chain-of-Thought (CoT) processes. Table 8 provides the detailed prompt templates used for these variants: Intuitive Mode (No-Thinking): The system prompt imposes strict negative constraints, forcing the model to provide an immediate judgment based on "first-order intuition" without intermediate analysis (e.g., "Directly output the score. Do NOT analyze, reason, or explain"). Analytical Mode (CoT): All negative constraints are removed, and the model is explicitly instructed to perform comprehensive review of the trajectory history and justify its scoring logic through step-by-step reasoning before arriving at final value. 20 Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx 2. Test-Time Compute Scaling (Reasoning Effort Control). For models with native reasoning-specialized architectures (e.g., GPT-OSS-20B), we modulate the depth of internal verification and search processes by adjusting the reasoning_effort parameter. This mechanism allows for the scaling of the test-time compute budget independently of the input prompt structure. In our experiments, we compare Baseline (Low reasoning effort) against an Enhanced (Medium reasoning effort) configuration. Notably, as these models generate reasoning within native reasoning content accessible via metadata, we can evaluate the logical derivation without requiring explicit analysis fields in the final text response. This allows us to maintain clean, standardized output formatconsisting solely of the reward scoreacross all models while still capturing the full depth of latent reasoning. By systematically varying the reasoning effort parameter, we can observe how increased latent search steps and self-correction cycles contribute to more precise grounding of the reward signal, effectively decoupling the models evaluative accuracy from its prompt-based instructions. Table 8. Detailed Baseline Prompt Templates. We strictly follow the provided prompt structures for both variants under the Full-History Protocol. The CoT variant (Part I) uses standard helper system prompt, while the Zero-Shot variant (Part II) explicitly injects [Without Thinking] constraint in the system prompt to suppress reasoning. Detailed Prompt Templates for Baseline Implementation PART I: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Variant (Reasoning Mode) System Prompt: You are helpful assistant. Please output the final answer in JSON format. User Prompt: You are an expert in task progress evaluation. Please judge the agents progress (0-100) based on the full interaction history. [Task Description] {task_description} [Interaction History] The following is the chronological log of the agents past actions and observations: {history_log} [Evaluation Requirements] 1. Analysis: Review the History to evaluate the progress strictly. 2. Scoring: Provide an integer score (0-100). Output strictly in JSON: {"reward": 50} PART II: Zero-Shot (No-Thinking) Variant (Direct Output) System Prompt: You are helpful assistant. Please output the final answer in JSON format. [Without Thinking] User Prompt: You are an expert in task progress evaluation. Judge the agents progress (0-100) based on the full interaction history provided below. [Task Description] {task_description} [Full Interaction History (From Start to Present)] The following is the chronological log of all agent actions and observations up to the current moment: {history_log} [Evaluation Requirements] Determine the progress score (0-100) based on the final state in the history. Do NOT analyze, reason, or explain. Rely on your intuition to give score immediately. [Output Format] Output strictly in JSON format: {"reward": 50} C.4 StateFactory Hyperparameters. To ensure experimental reproducibility and minimize stochastic variance, we utilize gpt-oss-20b as the primary reasoning backbone for all evaluative tasks. The model is deployed via the vLLM inference engine, with the sampling temperature fixed at near-deterministic 𝑇 = 0.01. We maintain consistent context window of 8,192 tokens. Embedding Model. Semantic similarity measures within StateFactory are computed using all-MiniLM-L6-v2. This specific encoder was selected based on our systematic ablation study (see Figure 5), where it demonstrated superior alignment fidelity for environment-specific state representations. Despite its compact 384-dimensional vector space, it outperformed larger alternatives in capturing the underlying semantic distance. 21 Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx C.4.1 StateFactory Inference Pipeline Our proposed method transforms raw observations into structured states via multi-stage pipeline. Table 9 details the Semantic State Factorization process (Modules & B). Table 10 details the Dynamic Goal Interpretation (Module C). Table 9. Semantic State Factorization Templates. This pipeline constitutes the first half of the StateFactory method. Module grounds raw observations into structured states, while Module filters and refines these states to maintain coherent, task-relevant history. StateFactory Pipeline Part I: Semantic State Factorization MODULE A: World State Extraction Template System Instruction: {system_instruction} Role: You are state extraction assistant. Your goal is to extract the current state of the agent based on the provided information, following the rules below: Rules: 1. Output Format: Output SINGLE JSON object. It must contain two keys: "thinking" and "current_state". (Perception & Grounding) "thinking": "For each object, cite the exact phrase from Observation that justifies its state. Verify that the object actually exists in Observation or Previous States. EXPLICITLY REJECT any information that appears ONLY in Previous Goal State." "current_state": {output_format_des} 2. Relevance: Only extract objects that are present in the Observation, or Previous States, and that are directly relevant to the Task Description. Include only those objects that actively contribute to task progress or are essential for understanding the current task state. Do not extract from Task Description or Previous Goal State. 3. Previous Goal State: This is provided ONLY for context verification. IT IS FORBIDDEN SOURCE. You must NOT copy, infer, or hallucinate any object or state from Previous Goal State into current_state. If an object is in the goal but hasnt been observed yet, ignore it. 4. Action Observation: The immediate feedback observed by the agent after performing an action. This may be None if no observable outcome occurs. 5. Previous States: Represents the accumulated task-relevant states from previous steps. Use it to maintain continuity in the agents world model: - If an object from Previous States appears in the current Observation, update its state with the most recent information. - If an object from Previous States does not appear in the current Observation, retain its previous state in Current State, assuming it remains unchanged. - Only include objects in Current State if they are either (a) newly observed and task-relevant, or (b) carried over from Previous States. - Do not include objects that are neither present in the current Observation nor in Previous States. Input: Task Description: {task_description}, Previous Goal State: {prev_goal_state}, Action: {last_action}, Observation: {observation}, Previous States: {prev_states} Output Example: {output_format} {output_format_des} MODULE B: Task-Relevant World State Extraction Template (Filtering & Refinement) System Instruction: {system_instruction} Role: You are task-relevant state extraction assistant. Given the historical task-related states (Previous States), the current steps new states (Current State), the actions taken (Action History), and the Task Description, extract and update the relevant world states according to the following rules: Rules: 1. Extract task-relevant states from the Current State. Exclude non-factual statements. Do not extract from Task Description. state is considered strongly relevant only if it directly or clearly indirectly affects whether the task can be completed. If uncertain about states relevance, exclude it. 2. Combine the results from Rule 1 with the previous historical states (Previous States) to form an updated set of task-relevant historical states. Note that Previous States may be None, in which case the output should be based exclusively on the results from Rule 1. 3. Refine the combined state set by preserving all states that are relevant to the current task, even if they are intermediate or partially superseded. Only remove or update state if it represents an outdated or incorrect version of the same information about specific object. In such cases, retain the most accurate and up-to-date state while discarding its obsolete counterparts. Avoid eliminating states merely because more specific or complete information has emerged, as long as the original states contribute to the task context or support traceability of reasoning. 4. Output Format: Only output the JSON contentno additional text, explanation, or formatting. Follow the specified JSON format below exactly: {output_format} {output_format_des} Input: Task Description: {task_description}, Previous States: {prev_states}, Current State: {current_state}, Action History: {action_history} 22 Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx Table 10. Dynamic Goal Interpretation Template. As the second stage of the StateFactory method, this component maintains persistent yet evolving definition of success, strictly distinguishing between the agents plans and the tasks goals. StateFactory Pipeline Part II: Dynamic Goal Interpretation MODULE C: Dynamic Goal Interpretation Template (Goal State Management) System Instruction: {system_instruction} Role: You are an expert in task goal state extraction. Your mission is to generate an Evolving Goal State: minimal, accurate JSON specification that defines only the FINAL, STATIC BLUEPRINT FOR TASK SUCCESS, derived exclusively from the Task Description. Core Principle: Goal vs. Plan (CRITICAL) Goal (Legal): concrete, factual state that must be objectively true at the moment of success AND is explicitly or implicitly required by the Task Description. This includes all explicit milestones in multi-step task (e.g., "First do A, then do B"). Plan / Blocker (Illegal): An intermediate step, prerequisite, enabling action, or blocker that YOU (the LLM) deduce is necessary, but is NOT one of the final goals listed in the Task Description. Constraint: Your Goal State MUST ONLY contain Goals. It MUST NEVER contain Plans or Blockers. Rules & Instructions: 1. Output Format: You must output only the JSON content. Do not include any additional text, explanations, comments, or markdown formatting. Adhere strictly to this JSON structure: Output SINGLE JSON object containing two keys: "thinking": step-by-step analysis string. First, analyze the Task Description to identify the ultimate success conditions. Second, verify that every goal attribute comes from the Task, NOT just because it exists in Current State. REJECT any state that appears solely because it is currently true in Current State. "goal_state": {output_format_des} Example of desired output structure: {output_format} {output_format_des} 2. Core Logic: Blueprint Creation (Step 0) a. Step 0: Complete Task Translation: - At Step 0, you MUST translate ALL final goal verbs and required milestones from the Task Description into their final physical states. - If task has multiple required parts (e.g., "First do A, then do B"), ALL parts must be translated into final states and included in the blueprint from the beginning. - This blueprint is the COMPLETE and FINAL definition of success. b. State-Change Filter: Use this only at Step 0 to exclude "trivial truths". 3. Core Logic: Blueprint Evolution (Step 1) a. GOAL PERSISTENCE: - The Goal State created at Step 0 is the static blueprint for success. - Once goal is added at Step 0, it MUST REMAIN for the entire episode. - DO NOT remove goals just because they are achieved in the Current State. b. STRICT IMMUTABILITY: - You are STRICTLY FORBIDDEN from adding new goals after Step 0. - The blueprint from Step 0 is considered complete. Do not add goals for plans, prerequisites, or blockers that you deduce. c. LEGAL EVOLUTION: The ONLY two legal modifications to the Goal State after Step 0 are: 1. Task Milestone Addition: Condition: The Task Description contains multi-step requirement (e.g., "First do A, then do B"), and the Observation confirms that step is now complete. Action: You are now allowed to add the goal for step to the Goal State. This is legal because the goal for originates from the task description. 2. Refinement / Anchoring: Condition: goal in the Goal State is generic, and the Observation first identifies its specific instance. Action: You MUST update the Goal State to "anchor" the generic goal to the specific, observed object. WARNING: Do not copy irrelevant attributes from Current State unless the Task specifically requires them. 4. Sources of Truth (Inputs) Task Description: Sole source of ultimate intent. Observation: Used only to ground generics or reveal implicit preconditions. Previous Goal State: The baseline to refinenever discard or contradict it without task-level justification. Current State & Action History: REFERENCE ONLY. Provide contextual world facts and recent action history that may inform phrasing, object references, or state granularity, but they must not introduce new goal requirements. Input: Task Description: {task_description}, Current State: {current_state}, Observation: {observation}, Action History: {action_history}, Previous Goal State: {prev_goal_state} Command: Now generate the updated GoalState based on the above rules. C.5 Ablation C.5.1 Representation To rigorously evaluate the impact of state representation, we implemented Representation-Controlled Ablation. We established baseline using the raw environment output and compared it against three levels of LLM-processed 23 Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx abstractions. Crucially, strictly controlling the representation format allows us to decouple the benefits of denoising from structuring. The four levels of granularity are defined as follows: Level 1: Unstructured Observations (Raw Input). This baseline utilizes the raw, noisy output from the environment directly as the state representation. It serves as the control group for "zero processing." Level 2: Textual States (Flat Denoising). The model outputs list of independent sentences. Critically, this level maintains the same unstructured format as Level 1 (flat natural language) but applies the relevance filtering constraint. Comparing Level 1 and Level 2 isolates the performance gain attributed solely to semantic denoising without the introduction of hierarchical structure. Level 3: Object-Centric States (Nested List). The model groups sentences under specific object keys. This introduces entity awareness and tests the value of hierarchical grouping, but retains the entangled attributes limitation within the descriptions. Level 4: Object-Attribute States (Full Factorization). The model strictly separates descriptions into atomic Key-Value pairs. This is the standard StateFactory configuration, which tests the value of complete attribute disentanglement. Table 11 presents the specific representation schemas used to enforce these constraints for Levels 1, 2, 3, and 4. Table 11. Prompt Variations for representation Structural Granularity. We utilized the same backbone and input context across all levels, varying only the Output Schema and Description Constraints to isolate the effect of structural depth. Each sentence must explicitly name its subject to ensure clarity and self-containment. Only entities that are explicitly present or clearly implied should be included. Description Constraint: Use key-value pairs to describe object attributes. The "value" (world_state_desc) must NOT assume the key is part of the sentence. It must be complete sentence with subject and verb. The "key" (state_name) must be short and summative. Output Schema: [ { "object": { "obj_desc": [ { "state_key": "world_state_desc" }, { "state_key": "world_state_desc" } ] } } ] 24 Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx C.5.2 LLM Backbone To isolate the contribution of the underlying LLMs capabilities, we conducted an ablation study by swapping the core backbone of the StateFactory pipeline while keeping all other distinct modules (e.g., prompt constraints, extraction protocols) constant. We evaluated distinct configurations varying in two dimensions: parametric scale (e.g., 14B vs. 30B) and inference strategy (Standard Instruct vs. Reasoning-Enhanced/Thinking modes). As shown in Table 16, both dimensions strongly correlate with alignment precision (measured by EPIC Distance ). Impact of Reasoning: Enabling thinking modes yields significant gains. For instance, shifting from GPT-OSS-20B (Low) to (Medium) improves the average EPIC distance from 0.36 to 0.30. Similarly, Qwen3-30B-Thinking (0.41) substantially outperforms its standard instruction-tuned counterpart (0.55), confirming that extended inference capability is crucial for decomposing complex environmental states into structured factors. Impact of Scale: Performance generally improves with parameter count within the same model family. Comparing Qwen3-14B (0.58) to Qwen3-30B (0.55), we observe natural improvement grounded in model size. Consequently, StateFactory is positioned to scale continuously with future advancements in both model size and reasoning strategies. C.5.3 Semantic Embedding As discussed in the main text, the reliability of StateFactory hinges on the embedding models ability to maintain Semantic Discriminabilitythe capacity to cluster semantically equivalent states while segregating distinct or contradictory ones. To quantify this, we utilize the Triplet-based Accuracy metric. Probing Dataset Construction. We derived synthetic probing dataset, 𝒟𝑝𝑟𝑜𝑏𝑒 , from StateFactorys intermediate outputs. For each unique state 𝑠𝑎𝑛𝑐 (Anchor), we generated triplet (𝑠𝑎𝑛𝑐 , 𝑠𝑝𝑜𝑠 , 𝑠𝑛𝑒 𝑔): Anchor (𝑠𝑎𝑛𝑐): raw factorized state description (e.g., "The apple is sliced"). Positive (𝑠𝑝𝑜𝑠): semantically equivalent but syntactically distinct paraphrase generated via an LLM. We explicitly instruct the model to vary vocabulary and structure while strictly preserving the truth value (e.g., "Slices of apple are present"). Negative (𝑠𝑛𝑒 𝑔): state sampled from the same episodes history describing different object or contradictory attribute (e.g., "The apple is whole" or "The table is dirty"). C.6 Utility for Agent Planning C.6.1 System-1 Policy Implementation Details To rigorously evaluate the efficacy of StateFactory, we implemented standard ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) agent as the primary baseline and the foundational System-1 component of our framework. The implementation follows the standard interleaving of thought generation and action execution, adapted for the specific constraints of AlfWorld, BlocksWorld, and ScienceWorld. Action Parsing and Error Recovery. The Language Model is queried to generate response in strict format: Thought: <reasoning> Action: <action>. We employ robust regular expression parser to extract the command: Syntactic Error Feedback: If the output format is malformed (e.g., missing the Action: prefix), the system intercepts the error and returns synthetic observation: Observation: Invalid Format. Please strictly follow the format Thought:... Action:.... Semantic Error Feedback: If the action is syntactically correct but physically impossible (e.g., trying to pick up table), the environments native error message is fed back to the agent, allowing for semantic self-correction. Action Space Exploration. To facilitate efficient exploration, we explicitly provide the agent with the list of currently valid actions (e.g., info[admissible_commands]) at each step. ReAct Baseline Prompt: AlfWorld (Household Tasks) Interact with household to solve task. Imagine you are an intelligent agent in household environment and your target is to perform actions to complete the task goal. At the beginning of your interactions, you will be given the detailed description of the current environment and your goal to accomplish and Task Knowledge to guide you to finish the task. For each of your turns, you will be given the observation of the last turn and the valid actions you can take right now (Valid Actions). You should first think about the current condition and plan for your future actions, then output your action in this turn. Your output must strictly follow this format: "Thought: your thoughts.nAction: your next action" Your response should use the following format: Thought: <your thoughts> Action: <your next action> Here is an example: You are in the middle of room. Looking quickly around you, you see bathtubbasin 1, cabinet 2, cabinet 1, countertop 1, garbagecan 1, handtowelholder 1, sinkbasin 1, toilet 1, toiletpaperhanger 1, and towelholder 1. Your task is to: put toiletpaper in toiletpaperhanger. Thought: need to place toilet paper roll on the toilet paper hanger. First, should check if theres already roll on the hanger or if its empty. Action: go to toiletpaperhanger 1 Observation: On the toiletpaperhanger 1, you see nothing. Thought: The toilet paper hanger is empty, so need to find new roll of toilet paper to put on it. Action: go to toilet 1 Observation: On the toilet 1, you see soapbottle 1, and toiletpaper 1. Thought: Ive found toilet paper roll on the toilet. should pick it up so can place it on the hanger. Action: take toiletpaper 1 from toilet 1 Observation: You pick up the toiletpaper 1 from the toilet 1. Thought: Now that have the toilet paper roll, need to return to the toilet paper hanger to install it. 27 Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx Action: go to toiletpaperhanger 1 Observation: On the toiletpaperhanger 1, you see nothing. Thought: Im back at the toilet paper hanger with the roll in hand. Its time to put the toilet paper on the hanger. Action: put toiletpaper 1 in/on toiletpaperhanger 1 - Now, the real task begins. ReAct Baseline Prompt: BlocksWorld (Spatial Logic) Interact with blocks world to solve stacking task. Imagine you are an intelligent planning agent in blocks world environment and your target is to perform actions to complete the task goal. At the beginning of your interactions, you will be given the detailed description of the current block configuration and your goal to accomplish. For each of your turns, you will be given the observation of the last turn and the valid actions you can take right now (Valid Actions). Each valid action is shown as: <ACTION_ID> -> <human-readable explanation> You should: 1. Use the explanation to understand what each action does. 2. Output ONLY the <ACTION_ID> (the part before "->") in your "Action:" line. Your response should use the following format: Thought: <your thoughts> Action: <ACTION_ID> Here is an example: Task: the green block is on top of the red block Observation: the green block is clear, the red block is on the table, the hand is empty Valid Actions: UNSTACK_A_B -> unstack the green block from on top of the red block PICK-UP_C -> pick up the blue block Thought: need to remove the green block from the red block. The action UNSTACK_A_B does exactly that. Action: UNSTACK_A_B - Now, the real task begins. ReAct Baseline Prompt: ScienceWorld (Scientific Discovery) You are an intelligent agent conducting scientific experiment in simulated environment. Your goal is to complete the given task by performing sequence of valid actions. The environment contains several rooms: kitchen, foundry, workshop, bathroom, outside, living room, bedroom, greenhouse, art studio, and hallway. For each of your turns, you will be given the observation of the last turn and the valid actions you can take right now (Valid Actions). You should first think about the current condition and plan for your future actions, then output your action in this turn. Your response should use the following format: Thought: <your thoughts> Action: <your next action> Example: Observation: You are in the kitchen. On the counter, you see beaker 1 and note 1. Valid Actions: [look around, examine beaker 1, read note 1, teleport to greenhouse, pick up beaker 1] Thought: should read the note firstit might contain instructions for the experiment. Action: read note 1 - Now, the real task begins. C.6.2 System-1 Policy Enhancement via StateFactory To overcome the ambiguity of sparse environmental signals in the standard ReAct baseline, we augment the System-1 policy with dense state-aware reward mechanism. Unlike the original ReAct loop which relies solely on textual observations, our framework explicitly quantifies the progress of each step via StateFactory. This allows the agent to evaluate the potential utility of candidate actions before finalizing its decision. The streamlined process operates as follows: 28 Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx Candidate Evaluation & State Abstraction. At each time step 𝑡, rather than blindly selecting an action, the agent first identifies set of candidate actions. For each candidate, we leverage the StateFactory engine to project the potential outcome into structured semantic state 𝑠𝑡+1. By using Dynamic Goal Interpretation prompts, we filter out environmental noise and focus on task-relevant state changes. Differential Reward Calculation. For each candidate transition, we compute an immediate, dense reward 𝑟𝑡 that reflects its contribution to the final goal 𝑔. The reward is defined by the gain in semantic proximity: 𝑟𝑡 = Score(𝑠𝑡+1, 𝑔) Score(𝑠𝑡 , 𝑔) 𝜆 I(Repetition) (15) This differential score captures the Relative Gain, positively rewarding actions that satisfy critical sub-goals (e.g., locating required tool) while penalizing redundant or cyclical behavior. Reward-Guided Decision Making. The calculated rewards {𝑟𝑡} are injected back into the ReAct context. The prompt is thus augmented from standard observation to (Candidate Action, Predicted Reward) assessment. This explicit feedback signal empowers the LLM to perform informed selectionprioritizing actions with high relative gain and pivoting away from unproductive strategies (low or negative reward) without requiring expensive lookahead simulations. We provide the prompts used to filter and propose the Top-𝐾 candidate actions in the subsequent sections. These prompts assist the ReAct policy in identifying logical next steps before simulation. Specifically, to balance exploration and efficiency, we set 𝐾 = 3 for AlfWorld and BlocksWorld, and 𝐾 = 5 for ScienceWorld due to its significantly larger and more complex action space. Action Selection Prompt: AlfWorld (Household Tasks) You are an expert agent playing text-based adventure game. Your goal is to complete the following Task: {task} Current Observation: {obs} Interaction History (Actions & Results): {history} Admissible Actions (Candidates): {candidates_with_ids} Your task is to identify the top {k} actions that are most likely to advance the game state. Selection Rules: 1. Analyze History: Check "Interaction History". Avoid actions that resulted in "Nothing happens" or revealed empty containers. Avoid loops. 2. Logical Continuity: Select actions that logically follow the observation (e.g., see target take it). 3. Exploration: If blocked, prioritize navigating to new areas. 4. Index Only: Return the ID numbers (e.g., 0, 1) only. Return JSON Format: { "reasoning": "selected_ids": "...", [id1, id2, ...] } Action Selection Prompt: ScienceWorld (Scientific Discovery) You are strategic planner for scientific agent. CONTEXT - Goal: {task} - Current Observation: {obs} - Recent History: {history} CANDIDATE ACTIONS Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx {candidates_with_ids} SELECTION STRATEGY 1. Analyze History: Do not repeat failed actions. If not in target room, prioritize navigation. 2. STRICT FOCUS PROTOCOL: - RISK: Focus on wrong object triggers -100 penalty. - SEQUENCE: Follow "First X, then Y" logic. Do not focus on if is not done. 3. Filter Noise: Ignore objects not mentioned in current goal step. OUTPUT FORMAT { "reasoning": "selected_ids": "Explain sequence logic...", [id1, id2, ...] } Action Selection Prompt: BlocksWorld (Spatial Logic) You are an expert agent solving Blocks World planning task. Your goal is to complete the following Task: {task} Context: - Observation: {obs} - History: {history} - Candidates: {candidates_with_ids} Format: [ID]: [ACTION_COMMAND] [description] Your Task: Select top {k} IDs based on: 1. Feasibility: Respect stacking rules. 2. Relevance: Move blocks toward target. 3. Repetition: Avoid failed actions. 4. Strategy: Free blocked blocks first. Return JSON Format: { "reasoning": "selected_ids": "...", [id1, id2, ...] } C.6.3 System-2 Planning via MCTS and World Models While the dense reward mechanism enhances the local policy, complex tasks often require counterfactual reasoningsimulating "what if" scenarios without committing to irreversible actions in the real environment. To achieve this, we implement System-2 planner using Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), grounded by pre-trained World Model [Li et al., 2025]. Generative Action Proposal (Top-𝐾). Unlike traditional RL environments where the action space is discrete and known, text-based environments feature an unbounded action space. During the expansion phase of MCTS, the World Model cannot list valid moves. Therefore, we utilize the Agent LLM as Generator. It analyzes the current observation and proposes the Top-𝐾 (typically 𝐾 = 3) most logical actions. As shown in the prompt below, we enforce Mental Scratchpad step, requiring the agent to explicitly verify inventory status and object visibility before generating commands, thereby reducing hallucinated actions. Simulation via World Model. Selected actions are executed in virtual environment simulated by LLaMA-based World Model [Li et al., 2025]. This model predicts the next textual observation 𝑜𝑡+1 given the history 𝐻𝑡 and action 𝑎𝑡. Truncated Rollout & Evaluation. major challenge in text simulation is "hallucination drift," where errors accumulate over long horizons. To mitigate this, we adopt conservative Single-Step Rollout strategy (𝑑 = 1). We prioritize accurate, immediate state evaluation over uncertain long-term planning. The simulated outcome is immediately scored by the 30 Reward Prediction with Factorized World States Sec 1: Intro Sec 2: Benchmark Sec 3: Method Sec 4: Experiments Sec 5: Related Works Sec 6: Conclusion Sec 7: Appx StateFactory engine, which calculates the reward based on the semantic state change. This score backpropagates to guide the root action selection. StateFactory as Semantic Value Function. The efficacy of our truncated planning (𝑑 = 1) heavily relies on the density of the evaluation signal. In traditional sparse-reward settings, single-step lookahead is typically insufficient to capture value, as the terminal goal remains distant. However, StateFactory bridges this gap by converting semantic state transitions into immediate scalar feedback, serving as robust heuristic for MCTS.
